accepted.question_text: Let Ik(T) = integral from 0 to T of |zeta(1/2 + it)|^(2k) dt. Assuming the Riemann Hypothesis, determine an explicit constant Ck, for each real k > 0, such that Ik(T) <= Ck T (log T)^(k^2) for all sufficiently large T.
accepted.context_brief: Explicit RH upper bound for zeta moments

evidence.quote: Give an explicit version of Harper’s [40] upper bound for Ik (T ), assuming the Riemann hypothesis. That is, for k > 0 determine Ck such that Ik (T ) <= Ck T (log T )^(k^2) for T sufficiently large.

accepted.question_text: Let omega(t) be a smooth compactly supported function supported in [c1 T, c2 T] with 0 < c1 < c2. Prove that the smoothed fourth moment of the Riemann zeta function satisfies integral over all real t of omega(t) |zeta(1/2 + it)|^4 dt = O(T^(1/2 + theta + epsilon)), where theta is an admissible upper bound toward the Ramanujan conjecture.

evidence.quote: Smoothed fourth moment. Let ω(t) be a smooth compactly supported function supported in [c1 T, c2 T ] where 0 < c1 < c2 . Show that integral omega(t)|zeta(1/2 + it)|^4 dt = O(T^(1/2 + theta + epsilon)) where theta is upper bound in Ramanujan’s conjecture.

accepted.question_text: Zavorotnyi proved that the fourth moment I2(T) of the Riemann zeta function has the asymptotic I2(T) = T P4(log T) + O(T^(2/3 + epsilon)), where P4 is a specific polynomial of degree 4. Improve the exponent in this error term.

evidence.quote: In 1989 Zavorotnyi proved that I2 (T ) = T P4 (log T ) + O(T^(2/3 + epsilon)), where P4 is a specific degree 4 polynomial. Improve the bound in the error term.

accepted.question_text: Suppose Ik(T) = T P_{k^2}(log T) + O(T^(1/2 + epsilon)) conjecturally, where P_{k^2} is a polynomial of degree k^2, and define the error term by Ek(T) = Ik(T) - T P_{k^2}(log T). Determine the optimal exponents theta_k for which Ek(T) = O(T^(theta_k + epsilon)) should hold for each integer k, especially for k >= 3, and decide whether the Multiple Dirichlet Series method can justify the correct conjectural values.

evidence.quote: It would be desirable to come up with a conjecture for optimal constants θk such that Ek (T ) = O(T^(θk + epsilon)) holds for k in N. N. Ng believes that θ1 = 1/4 and θ2 = 1/2 are the correct answers for k = 1, 2. What about k >= 3? Both Ivic and Motohashi suggested θ3 = 3/4. Can the Multiple Dirichlet Series method be used to derive an appropriate conjecture?

accepted.question_text: For the shifted moment Mk(T, alpha) = integral from 0 to T of the product over i of |zeta(1/2 + t + alpha_i)|^(2k_i) dt, Chandee proved an upper bound of the shape Mk(T, alpha) <<_{k,epsilon} T (log T)^(k_1^2 + ... + k_m^2 + epsilon) times the product over i < j of min{1/|alpha_i - alpha_j|, log T}^{2 k_i k_j}, under the stated hypotheses on the shifts alpha_i(T). Use Harper’s method to remove the epsilon from this bound.

evidence.quote: Use Harper’s method [40] to remove the ε. Note that the case of two shifts (m = 2 and k1 = k2 ) has been dealt with in [60].
Michael Curran [25] has solved this problem.

accepted.question_text: Soundararajan proved the lower bound I3(T) >= (20.26 a3 / 9!) (1 + o(1)) T (log T)^9 for sufficiently large T. Improve the constant 20.26, ideally all the way to the conjecturally best value 42, and likewise improve the corresponding lower-bound constants for Ik(T) when k > 3.
accepted.context_brief: Lower bounds for cubic and higher moments

evidence.quote: Soundararajan [65] proved the following lower bound for I3 (T ): I3 (T ) >= 20.26 a3 / 9! (1 + o(1)) T (log T )^9, for T sufficiently large. Improve the value of 20.26. The best possible result would be 42. (Likewise improve the lower bounds for Ik (T ) for k > 3.)

accepted.question_text: Assuming the ternary additive divisor conjecture, prove an omega theorem for the sixth moment I3(T) of the Riemann zeta function by showing that there exists Theta > 0 such that I3(T) = T P9(log T) + Omega(T^Theta), where P9 is a polynomial of degree 9.

evidence.quote: Is it possible to prove an omega theorem for I3 (T ) assuming the ternary additive divisor conjecture? Prove that there exists Θ > 0, such that I3 (T ) = T P9 (log T ) + Ω(T^Θ) where P9 is a certain degree nine polynomial.

accepted.question_text: Assume the Riemann Hypothesis and a smoothed quaternary additive divisor conjecture. Starting from the conjectured asymptotic I4(T) ~ C4 T (log T)^16 for the eighth moment of the Riemann zeta function, derive the full main term up to an error of size o(T) or a power saving, obtain a shifted analogue, and asymptotically evaluate the smoothed shifted integral of product_{j=1}^4 zeta(1/2 + a_j + it) zeta(1/2 + b_j - it) when |a_j| and |b_j| are << 1/log T and omega is supported in [c1 T, c2 T].
accepted.context_brief: Full main term for the eighth moment

evidence.quote: Under the same assumptions, obtain the full main term, up to an error term of size o(T ) or a power savings error term. Obtain a shifted version of this result. Under the same assumptions, asymptotically evaluate integral omega(t) product_{j=1}^4 zeta(1/2 + a_j + it) zeta(1/2 + b_j - it) dt where a_j, b_j are complex numbers satisfying |a_j|, |b_j| << 1/log T.

accepted.question_text: The CFKRS recipe gives a conjectural full asymptotic formula for Ik(T). Make this recipe rigorous for the fourth moment, by proving the asymptotic formula for I2(T) with a power-saving error term using the recipe method itself.

evidence.quote: Can the ‘recipe’ be made rigorous in the case of the fourth moment? That is, can we give a new proof of the asymptotic formula with power savings error term for I2 (T ) following the ‘recipe’ method.

accepted.question_text: Can the conjectural lower-order terms for Ik(T), attributed to CFKRS and to Diaconu-Goldfeld-Hoffstein, be recovered by refining the splitting conjecture in the Gonek-Hughes-Keating hybrid Euler-Hadamard product?

evidence.quote: Can the conjectural lower order terms for Ik (T ) (due to CFKRS [24] and Diaconu-Goldfeld-Hoffstein [30]) be recovered by refining the splitting conjecture in the Gonek-Hughes-Keating [37] hybrid Euler-Hadamard product?

accepted.question_text: Let f run over holomorphic Hecke eigenforms of weight k_f <= T. Prove asymptotic formulas, with the best possible error terms, for the third and fourth moments of the central values L(1/2, f), normalized by L(1, ad f), in particular aiming for sums of the shape T^2 P3(log T) + O_epsilon(T^(1+epsilon)) for the cubic moment and T^2 P6(log T) + O_epsilon(T^(4/3+epsilon)) for the quartic moment.

evidence.quote: Prove third and fourth moment asymptotics with best possible error terms for L(1/2, f ) as f runs over holomorphic Hecke eigenforms of weight kf <= T. It should be possible to show, using current technology, that ... = T^2 P3(log T) + O_epsilon(T^(1+epsilon)) and ... = T^2 P6(log T) + O_epsilon(T^(4/3+epsilon)).

accepted.question_text: Heath-Brown proved a Weyl-strength twelfth-moment bound for the Riemann zeta function, Jutila proved an analogous sixth-moment result in the t-aspect for L(1/2 + it, f) with f a Hecke-Maass cusp form, and complementary twelfth-moment results are known for Dirichlet L-functions in the level aspect. Prove the analogous Weyl-strength bound for the sixth moment of L(1/2, f tensor chi).
accepted.context_brief: Sixth moment of twists of Maass forms

evidence.quote: Can one also prove analogous bounds for the sixth moment of L(1/2, f ⊗ χ)?

accepted.question_text: Jutila proved a Weyl-strength twelfth-moment bound for Hecke-Maass cusp forms of level 1 with spectral parameter at most T. Prove a level-aspect analogue in which one sums over Hecke-Maass cusp forms of level q and bounded spectral parameter.

evidence.quote: Can one prove a level-aspect analogue of this, where one sums over Hecke–Maass cusp forms of level q and bounded spectral parameter?

accepted.question_text: For a Hecke-Maass cusp form f of level 1 and fixed spectral parameter t_f, Good proved that the second moment in t has the form integral from 0 to T of |L(1/2 + it, f)|^2 dt = T P1(log T) + O_{t_f,epsilon}(T^(2/3 + epsilon)). Make the dependence on t_f in the error term explicit, determine whether the error can be made independent of t_f when T >= t_f^(3/2 + delta) for some delta > 0, and obtain the analogous statement for holomorphic Hecke eigenforms of weight k_f.
accepted.context_brief: Uniformity in spectral or weight aspect

evidence.quote: Crucially, the dependence on tf in the error term is not made explicit. Can one make this dependence explicit? In particular, does this error term not depend on tf if, say, T >= tf^(3/2+delta) for some delta > 0? How about for holomorphic Hecke eigenforms of weight kf ?

accepted.question_text: Use the work of Humphries and Khan to compute weight-aspect upper bounds for the sixth moment of L(1/2, f tensor g) when f and g vary over primitive forms of level 1 and weights k and ell, under the conditions |k - ell| = O(1) and K < k + ell <= 2K.
accepted.context_brief: Weight-aspect sixth moment for Rankin-Selberg L-values

evidence.quote: Use the recent work of Humphries-Khan [42] to compute weight aspect upper bounds for the 6th moment of L(1/2, f ⊗ g) where f and g vary over the set of primitive forms of level 1 and weights k and ℓ respectively, with |k − ℓ| = O(1) and K < k + ℓ <= 2K.

accepted.question_text: Let chi_{8d} denote the quadratic character attached to the fundamental discriminant 8d, with d ranging over odd squarefree integers 0 < d < X. Young proved a first-moment asymptotic of the form sum L(1/2, chi_{8d}) = X P(log X) + O(X^(1/2 + epsilon)). Improve this by reducing the exponent 1/2, ideally toward a secondary term of size c X^(1/4) plus a smaller error.
accepted.context_brief: First moment of quadratic Dirichlet L-functions

evidence.quote: Matthew Young [71] proved that sum_{0<d<X} L(1/2, χ8d) = X P(log X) + O(X^(1/2 + epsilon)). Alexandra Florea has suggested that sum_{0<d<X} L(1/2, χ8d) = X P(log X) + c X^(1/4) + O(X^ϑ) may be true for some c in R and ϑ in (0, 1/4). The goal would be to improve (18) by reducing the exponent 1/2.

accepted.question_text: Let chi_{8d} denote the quadratic character attached to the fundamental discriminant 8d, with d ranging over odd squarefree integers 0 < d < X. Determine a polynomial P of degree 10 such that sum L(1/2, chi_{8d})^4 = X P(log X) + o(X), decide whether the error term can be improved to a power saving, and determine whether the Multiple Dirichlet Series method can recover at least the initial coefficients of P.
accepted.context_brief: Fourth moment of quadratic Dirichlet L-functions

evidence.quote: Determine a polynomial P of degree 10 such that sum_{0<d<X} L(1/2, χ8d)^4 = X P(log X) + o(X) where d ranges through odd square-free numbers and χ8d denotes the quadratic character associated to the fundamental discriminant 8d. Can the error term o(X) be replaced by a power savings error term? Can the Multiple Dirichlet series method be used to obtain the first coefficients of P?

accepted.question_text: Fix a prime p > 2 and a power q of p. Let L(u, f, psi) be the Artin-Schreier L-function attached to a rational function f in F_q(x) and an additive character psi. For the families AS_d^ord = {f = h/g : h, g in F_q[x], (g,h)=1, g squarefree, deg f = max(deg h, deg g) = d, deg g in {d, d-1}}, AS_d^0 = {f in F_q[x] : deg f = d, (d,p)=1}, and AS_d^{0,odd} = {f in F_q[x] : deg f = d, f(x) = -f(-x), (d,2p)=1}, with q fixed and d -> infinity and with M_k denoting the k-th moment of L(q^(1/2), f, psi), compute the listed low moments and extend the 1-level density of zeros beyond the currently stated support ranges: for AS_d^ord, compute M1 through M6 and prove 1-level density for support of phi-hat inside (-1-delta, 1+delta) or (-2-delta, 2+delta); for AS_d^0, compute M1 through M4 and prove 1-level density for support inside (-(2-2/p)-delta, 2-2/p+delta); for AS_d^{0,odd}, compute M1 and M2 and prove 1-level density for support inside (-(1-1/p)-delta, 1-1/p+delta).

evidence.quote: Compute moments for families of Artin-Schreier L-functions and improve results on 1-level zero density.
AS ord : compute 1-level density for supp φ̂ ⊂ (-1 - δ, 1 + δ) for some δ > 0 or (-2 - δ, 2 + δ), for some δ > 0. Compute M1, M2, M3, M4, M5, M6.
AS 0 : compute 1-level density for supp φ̂ ⊂ (-(2 - 2/p) - δ, 2 - 2/p + δ). Compute M1, M2, M3, M4. AS 0,odd : compute 1-level density for supp φ̂ ⊂ (-(1 - 1/p) - δ, 1 - 1/p + δ). Compute M1, M2.

accepted.question_text: Investigate how zero-density estimates depend on available moment bounds for the Riemann zeta function. In particular, Ingham’s method uses the fourth moment integral over [T, 2T] of |zeta(1/2 + it)|^4 dt; determine whether other nontrivial moments, such as Heath-Brown’s twelfth-moment estimate, can be used in zero-density arguments, and whether analogous arguments can be generalized to other L-functions.

evidence.quote: Investigate the dependence of zero-density on moments of the zeta-function? More specifically, Ingham’s zero-density estimate makes use of the fourth moment ... Can we use other non-trivial moments (like, say, Heath-Brown’s estimate of the 12th moment) in such zero-density results, and can we generalise this to other L-functions?

accepted.question_text: Chandee and Soundararajan proved, under the Riemann Hypothesis, that |zeta(1/2 + it)| is bounded by exp((C0 + o(1)) log t / log log t) with C0 = (log 2)/2. Reduce the constant C0 in this bound.

evidence.quote: Chandee and Soundararajan [15] proved that the Riemann Hypothesis implies |ζ(1/2 + it)| << exp((C0 + o(1)) log t / log log t) with C0 = log 2 / 2 = 0.1505 ... . Reduce the value of C0.

accepted.question_text: Let S(t) = (1/pi) arg zeta(1/2 + it). Carneiro, Chandee, and Milinovich proved, under the Riemann Hypothesis, that |S(t)| <= (C0 + o(1)) log t / log log t with C0 = 1/4. Reduce the constant C0 in this estimate.

evidence.quote: Let S(t) = π1 arg ζ(1/2 + it). Carneiro, Chandee, and Milinovich [12] proved that the Riemann hypothesis implies |S(t)| <= (C0 + o(1)) · log t / log log t with C0 = 1/4. Reduce the value of C0.

accepted.question_text: Let f be a Hecke-Maass cusp form of level 1 with spectral parameter t_f. Prove the Weyl-strength subconvex estimate L(1/2 + it, f) <<_epsilon ((1 + |t_f - t|)(1 + |t_f + t|))^(1/6 + epsilon) uniformly, including the conductor-dropping regimes where t_f - t or t_f + t is small relative to max{t_f, |t|}.

evidence.quote: Let f be a Hecke–Maass cusp form of level 1. Prove the Weyl-strength subconvex bound L(1/2 + it, f ) <<_epsilon ((1 + |tf − t|)(1 + |tf + t|))^(1/6+epsilon). This is known ... away from the conductor-dropping regimes ... Michel-Venkatesh prove a subconvex bound in the conductor-dropping regime, but the exponent is not explicit.

accepted.question_text: For k >= 3, let D_{k,2}(x, h) = sum_{n<=x} d_k(n) d_2(n+h), and let M_{k,2}(x, h) denote the main term in the known asymptotic formula. Prove an omega theorem for the error D_{k,2}(x, h) - M_{k,2}(x, h).
accepted.context_brief: Omega theorem for shifted divisor sums

evidence.quote: Topacogullari [68], [70] and Drappeau [33] have proven asymptotic formulae for Dk,2(x, h). Prove an omega theorem for Dk,2(x, h)−Mk,2 (x, h) for k >= 3 where Mk,2 (x, h) is the main term in the asymptotic formula.

accepted.question_text: Topacogullari proved that for 1 <= h << x^(2/3), D_{3,2}(x, h) = x P3(log x) + O(x^(8/9 + epsilon)), where P3 is a degree-3 polynomial whose coefficients depend on h, and analogous results are known for D_{2,3}(x, h). Improve both the admissible range of h and the size of the error term, derive exact simple formulas for the coefficients of P3, and determine numerically whether |D_{3,2}(x, h) - x P3(log x)| has size about sqrt(x).

evidence.quote: Topacogullari [68] proved that for 1 <= h << x^(2/3) D3,2 (x, h) = xP3 (log x) + O(x^(8/9 + epsilon)) for a certain degree 3 polynomial where the coefficients of P3 depend on h.

Improve the range of h and the size of the error term. Compute exact simple formulae for the coefficients of P3. Numerically check the size of |D3,2 (x, h)−xP3 (log x)|. Is the error term of size sqrt(x)?

accepted.question_text: Asymptotically evaluate each of the following additive-divisor correlations: sum_{n<=x} d3(n) d3(n+1); sum_{n<=x} d(n) d(n+1) d(n+2); for every kappa in (0,1), sum_{n<=x} d_kappa(n) d(n+1) d(n+2); and for every kappa in (2,3), sum_{n<=x} d_kappa(n) d3(n+1).

evidence.quote: Problems on additive divisor sums: Asymptotically evaluate sum d3 (n)d3 (n + 1). Asymptotically evaluate sum d(n)d(n + 1)d(n + 2). Let κ in (0, 1). Asymptotically evaluate sum dκ (n)d(n + 1)d(n + 2). Let κ in (2, 3). Asymptotically evaluate sum dκ (n)d3 (n + 1).

accepted.question_text: For distinct positive integers h1, ..., hr with r >= 3 and integers k1, ..., kr >= 1, find a conjectural asymptotic formula for sum_{n<=x} d_{k1}(n+h1) d_{k2}(n+h2) ... d_{kr}(n+hr), analogous to the Hardy-Littlewood style conjectural asymptotic for sum_{n<=x} Lambda(n+h1) ... Lambda(n+hr).

evidence.quote: Find a (conjectural) asymptotic formula for sum_{n<=x} dk1 (n + h1 )dk2 (n + h2 ) · · · dkr (n + hr ) where (k1 , . . . , kr ) in N^r and h1 , . . . , hr are distinct positive integers with r >= 3. This would be analogous to the conjectural asymptotic formula for sum_{n<=x} Lambda(n + h1 ) · · · Lambda(n + hr ).

accepted.question_text: Fix an integer k >= 3 and a small epsilon_0 >= 0. Asymptotically evaluate the mean value integral from 0 to T of (sum_{m <= T^(2+epsilon_0)} d_k(m) / m^(1/2+it)) (sum_{n <= T^(2+epsilon_0)} d(n) / n^(1/2-it)) dt.

evidence.quote: Let k in N and k >= 3. For some small ǫ0 >= 0 asymptotically evaluate integral from 0 to T of sum_{m<=T^(2+ǫ0)} d_k(m)/m^(1/2+it) times sum_{n<=T^(2+ǫ0)} d(n)/n^(1/2-it) dt.

accepted.question_text: Let g be a primitive cusp form of weight k for Gamma_0(N), normalized so that its first Fourier coefficient is 1 and written as g(z) = sum_{n>=0} lambda_g(n) n^((k-1)/2) e(nz). Let F(x,y) be a smooth weight function supported on (X/4, 4X] x [Y/4, 4Y], satisfying the derivative bounds stated in the paper, and define the shifted convolution sum D_g(ell_1, ell_2; h) = sum_{ell_1 m +/- ell_2 n = h} lambda_g(n) lambda_g(m) F(ell_1 m, ell_2 n) for coprime ell_1 and ell_2. Determine how the explicit constant in the known asymptotic upper bounds depends on k and N, and in the full modular-group case prove a weight-uniform asymptotic upper bound useful for Rankin-Selberg moment problems with large weights.
accepted.context_brief: Shifted convolution sums for cusp forms

evidence.quote: Consider the shifted convolution sum Dg (ℓ1 , ℓ2 ; h) = sum_{ℓ1 m±ℓ2 n=h} λg (n)λg (m)F (ℓ1 m, ℓ2 n), where ℓ1 and ℓ2 are coprime.

Determine the dependence of the explicit constant on k and N. For a primitive cusp form g of weight k for the full modular group, establish an asymptotic upper bound that is uniform in the weight aspect.

needs_review.question_text_raw: Remove the integral in t from Chandee-Li’s work and give an asymptotic formula for 2/phi(q) times a parity-restricted character sum over f in H_chi of |L(1/2, f)|^6.

evidence.quote: Remove the integral in t from Chandee-Li’s work and give an asymptotic formula for ... See work of Chandee-Li-Matomaki-Radziwill on the sixth moment of Dirichlet L-functions.

needs_review.question_text_raw: Consider the family of Rankin-Selberg L-functions L(1/2, f tensor g) where g is fixed and f varies over primitive forms of weight k and level p^nu as nu -> infinity. Establish upper bounds for third and fourth moments of this family following the work in [?] and [?].

evidence.quote: Consider the family of Rankin-Selberg L-functions L(1/2, f ⊗ g) where g is fixed and f varies over the set of primitive forms of weight k and level p^ν as ν -> ∞. Establish upper bounds for third and fourth moments of this family following the work in [?] and [?].

trace.notes: The paper has a brief introduction, a notation section on page 5 defining Ik(T), d_k(n), and D_{k,ell}(x,h), then a problem list split across pages 5-12 into three major regions: moments of the Riemann zeta function and related L-functions (Problems 1-21), bounds for L-functions (Problems 22-24), and additive divisor sums/shifted convolution sums (Problems 25-30). No theorem/proposition/lemma index was used; the likely open-problem regions are exactly the numbered problem items and their bullet subitems.
Extracted numbered candidates q_001 through q_021 from the PROBLEMS section covering moments of zeta, moments of automorphic L-functions, Artin-Schreier families, and zero-density questions.
Extracted numbered candidates q_022 through q_024 from the BOUNDS FOR L-FUNCTIONS section.
Extracted numbered candidates q_025 through q_030 from the ADDITIVE DIVISOR SUMS / SHIFTED CONVOLUTION SUMS section.
